content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 25,
1993
Toby Bowler,
Esq.
572 North Arrowhead,
Suite 100
San Bernardino,
CA
92401
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1-93—287
Dear Mr.
Bowler:
You have requested advice on behalf of San Bernardino
Planning Commissioner Ralph Affaitati concerning his duties under
the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the “Act”) .
We are treating your request as one for informal
assistance since your request does not pertain to a particular
governmental decision. 2
QUESTION
May Planning Commissioner Affaitati contact city employees in
departments such as building,
engineering,
and water in his
private capacity as a general building contractor concerning his
construction projects?
CONCLUS ION
The Act prohibits a public official who has a material
financial interest in a decision before his or her agency from
attempting to use his or her official position to influence that
decision.
Therefore,
Commissioner Affaitati may not appear before
his own agency or contact city planning staff regarding a matter
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000
seq.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c).)
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
1—93-287
Page
2
which is,
or may come, before his own agency, or which is
appealable to,
and may be reconsidered by, the planning
commission.
However, generally speaking,
a public official can represent
his or her private interests before an agency not under the
budgetary or appointive control of the official’s own agency,
provided he or she does not act or purport to act on behalf of his
or her own agency.
FACTS
Commissioner Affaitati is a general building contractor who
serves on the city’s planning commission.
He also is the sole
owner of his general contracting business.
In the performance of his duties as a general contractor, he
may be required to have direct contact with city employees in
departments such as building, engineering,
and water on matters
unrelated to planning commission decisions.
For example,
he may
need to call the building department to request inspection for a
given phase of a construction project,
and later have direct
contact with the inspector who visits the site.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.
An official has a financial interest in a decision that
will have a foreseeable and material financial effect, different
from the effect on the general public,
on the official or on the
official’s immediate family or on the following:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
***
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.


Our File No.
1-93-287
Page
3
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a),
(c),
and (d).
Commissioner Affaitati is a public official.
(Section
82048.)
Therefore, he is disqualified from participating in any
decision that will have a reasonably foreseeable and material
financial effect on any of his economic interests,
including his
general contracting business or a source of income to him of $250
or more.
(Sections 82005,
87100,
and 87103.)
Disqualification generally means that the official cannot
make, participate in making, or otherwise try to influence a
decision when acting within the authority of his position.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18700; Regulation 18700.1; Levinger
Advice Letter,
No.
1—88—328; West Advice Letter,
No.
A-88—413.)
Regulation 18700.1 provides that even conduct in an
official’s private capacity may be characterized as using his
official position to influence a decision.
Regulation 18700.1 provides in relevant part:
(a)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official’s agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts,
or
appears before,
or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.
Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to,
appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.
****
(c)
With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an agency not covered by
subsection
(a), the official is attempting to use
his or her official position to influence the
decision if, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, the official acts or purports to act on
behalf of,
or as the representative of, his or her
agency to any member, officer,
employee or


Our File No.
1—93—287
Page
4
consultant of an agency.
Such actions include, but
are not limited to the use of official stationery.
Regulation 18700.1(a)
and (c).
(Emphasis added.)
You have not provided us with any facts which indicate
whether the contacts Mr. Affaitati will be making concern
governmental decisions which are subject to his agency’s budgetary
or appointive control.
Therefore,
we are providing you general
guidance with respect to governmental decisions which may or may
not be subject to his agency’s budgetary or appointive control.
Pursuant to subdivision (a),
Mr. Affaitati may not appear
before his own agency
3
(the planning commission)
or make any
contact with the city planning staff regarding a matter which is,
or may come, before the planning commission.
Such appearances or
contacts before the planning commission would be an improper
attempt to use his official position to influence a decision.
This would include any word or deed to attempt to affect or
further the agency’s decision.
(Collins Advice Letter,
No.
78—06—074.)
However, Regulation 18700(d) (2)
and Regulation 18700.1(b) (1),
both allow a public official to appear,
as any member of the
general public,
before an agency in the course of its prescribed
governmental function, to represent himself or herself on matters
related solely to the official’s personal interests.
A “personal
interest” includes,
in part,
a business entity wholly owned by the
official or members of the official’s immediate family,
or a busi
ness entity over which the official exercises sole direction and
control,
or over which the official and the official’s spouse
jointly exercise sole direction and control.
Since Mr. Affaitati is the sole proprietor of his business,
the exclusions provided for under Regulation 18700(d) (2)
and
Regulation 18700.1(b) (1)
would apply.
However, where the
exception would apply, the official’s comments must be limited to
his personal interests,
and care should be taken to clarify that
he is not acting in any official capacity.
(Larsen Advice Letter,
No.
A-87-151.)
Thus, the exception would not permit Commissioner
Affaitati to appear before the planning commission to represent
any other person’s interests, including his clients.
(Gallagher
Advice Letter,
No.
1—93—205.)
Section 82041 defines “local government agency” as a county,
city, or district of any kind including “any department, division,
bureau, office,
board,
commission or other agency of the
foregoing.”
Generally, the Commission has treated different
governmental departments as different agencies.
For example,
a
planning commission is a distinct agency from the city council or
the department of city planning.
(Stout Advice Letter,
No.
1—88—313.)


Our File No.
1-93—287
Page 5
You indicate in your letter that Commissioner Affaitati is
concerned about situations where he would be acting in his private
capacity in making contacts before employees of the building,
engineering,
and water departments of the city.
Assuming the
contacts you are concerned about concern decisions which are not
under his agency’s budgetary or appointive control,
as long as
Mr. Affaitati does not act or purport to represent the planning
commission,
4 he is not restricted in his communication with other
city departments relating to those decisions.
I trust this letter has provided you with guidance regarding
the duties and responsibilities of Planning Commissioner Affaitati
under the Act.
If you have any further questions regarding these
matters, please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-59O1.
Sincerely,
Wayne Ordos
Executive Director
t__
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
‘
Purporting to represent the planning commission include, but
are not limited to, using official stationery to communicate with
any member, officer,
employee,
or consultant of the agency.
(Regulation 18700 (c).)
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lO per page.
